messages.text: Hello good afternoon. My name is [Patient] and I am trying to look for a private based endometriosis specialist in Spain.

Hi, thanks for reaching out.

We help connect people with trusted women's health specialists for diagnosis, symptom management, and surgical care.

To help us guide you, it would be helpful to know:

* What condition or symptoms you're seeking help for
* Any relevant history (diagnosis, surgeries, scans, treatments)
* Your location and how far you'd be willing to travel
* Whether you have insurance or would be self-funding

If you're considering surgery, you can also let us know whether you're exploring private or NHS options.

A member of our team will review and get back to you shortly.

If this is a medical emergency, please contact 999.

We do not provide medical diagnoses or medical advice.

Hi Koryn, unfortunately we don't yet have coverage on endometriosis specialists based in Spain. There are limited things we can do to help you on that front. But if you for example would like us to make you a case summary that you can take to a consultant or make a checklist of questions to ask, we'd be happy to help you do that.
A checklist would be helpful.
There’s lots of specialists in Barcelona but its important to find the right one.

It certainly is very important to find the right specialist. To help you generate a checklist and perhaps think of a few criteria to look for in a specialist, can you just walk me through a bit about your symptoms, your journey with endometriosis so far, for example, any diagnosis, scans, surgeries, treatments?

So I have had surgery already back in the USA. It was in December of 2021 and I had some relief afterwards but the symptoms have progressively gotten worse. I’ve done an MRI and also had a new IUD Morena inserted. Since then I also started a medication called Ryego that’s put my body into menopause and things are worse than ever.
I keep experiencing hip pain that goes down to my legs and I can’t walk and also rectal pain. I am about to lose my job because of missing work.
The anti-inflammatories they give me basically aren’t working.
I’ve changed my diet and that only helps to an extent.
My current specialist is putting a lot of pressure for me to get pregnant keeps insisting on just using ant-inflammatory pills.
Thanks for sharing these information. Sorry to hear that you've been through a lot already.

Can I ask,  when you had your surgery in 2021, was it confirmed as endometriosis, and do you know if it was an excision surgery (where the disease is cut out) or ablation (where it's burned off)?
* Did the MRI you had show any specific areas of endometriosis, for example, on your bowel or other organs?
* Do you recall if the surgeon who performed your first surgery was an endometriosis specialist, or a general gynaecologist?
The MRI was after my surgery but it showed that the endometriosis was where my appendix was. They removed it during my surgery due to adhesions.
The surgeon was an endometriosis specialist that did a robotic surgery. He’s since retired.
So has the specialist that ordered the MRI. The current specialist claims she has no idea where it is in my body but also has not asked for a new MRI.

My current specialist refuses to do another surgery until I’ve had all the children I wish to have. At this point, I am not comfortable with a pregnancy due to the pain and the possibility of still being in pain afterwards.

Thank you, and just a few more questions. 

Does the hip/leg pain travel down the leg or come with numbness/tingling?

Do you get pain or urgency with bowel movements?

Do you know if your MRI was done specifically for endometriosis (specialist scan)?

Are you mainly looking to prioritise pain relief rather than fertility right now?

It goes down to the thigh but no farther than that. I don’t think I’ve felt numbness or anything like that. I feel urgency with bowel movements or if I am holding in urine. Sometimes that causes discomfort. 
As far as I know of the scan was specifically for endometriosis. 
At this point I want to prioritise pain relief and just have a quality of life.
Fertility is important as well but this is to the point where it’s really affecting my daily life.
It also causes headaches that almost feel like a migraine. I didn’t know that was a symptom till recently.

Thank you for sharing all of that — I’m really sorry you’re going through this, especially with how much it’s affecting your day-to-day life. Based on everything you’ve told us, we’ve put together a clear summary of your situation and what to look for in a specialist, so you can move forward with the right level of care.

Case summary:
You have a confirmed history of endometriosis, previously treated with robotic excision surgery in December 2021, with initial relief but now progressively worsening symptoms. Your current symptoms include significant pelvic pain, hip pain radiating into the thigh, rectal pain, and bowel urgency, all of which are now severely impacting your ability to walk and work. You’ve had an MRI (reported as endometriosis-focused), a Mirena coil inserted, and are currently on Ryeqo, but your symptoms have worsened despite this. Your previous surgeon has retired, and your current specialist has not pursued further imaging or surgical options and is instead recommending pregnancy, which you’re not comfortable with given your current level of pain. At this stage, your priority is pain relief and quality of life, while still keeping fertility as a secondary consideration.

What to look for in a specialist:

* An advanced endometriosis excision surgeon (not just a general gynaecologist), with experience in complex or repeat surgery

* Regular experience treating deep infiltrating endometriosis (DIE), especially involving the bowel or rectal area

* Access to a multidisciplinary team, particularly with colorectal surgeons for bowel-related disease

* A practice that uses specialist imaging (endometriosis MRI or expert ultrasound) to properly map disease before treatment

* A doctor who is comfortable balancing pain relief and fertility, rather than delaying care solely for pregnancy

* Ideally based within a dedicated endometriosis centre that manages complex cases routinely

If you’d like, you can also share your current doctor’s name and we can help sense-check whether they’re the right fit for this level of care.
Hopefully, this makes it easier for when you search for a specialist that matches your need in Barcelona.
If there is anything else that I can do to be helpful, please let me know.

Thank you so much for the information. I don’t have the names of the specialist at the public hospital but I do have a name of a specialist that I have a video call with on Friday. She’s based in Barcelona.
Dra. María Eulalia Fernández Montolí
She has 20 years of experience with endometriosis supposedly and allowed video appointments. I thought that would be best for a first appointment before we go in person.
Also, I agree either way all of this checklist. I’m a bit concerned that this is potentially near my bowel or rectal area and possibly the bladder.

Thank you for sharing this — she looks like a very experienced and well-qualified gynaecologist with a strong academic and clinical background, and it’s a positive that she is involved in endometriosis care.

That said, based on your symptoms (especially the rectal pain, bowel urgency, and how much this is affecting your mobility), your case likely falls into a more complex category of endometriosis that often requires a surgeon who regularly treats deep/infiltrating disease, particularly involving the bowel.

From what we can see, it’s not fully clear whether she routinely manages that level of complexity or works as part of a multidisciplinary surgical team (e.g. with colorectal specialists), which is usually important in cases like yours.

She could still be helpful as a second opinion or for coordinating further imaging, but it may also be worth ensuring you explore a specialist who regularly handles advanced endometriosis cases.

If you do speak with her, it could be helpful to ask a few quick questions to understand if she’s the right fit for your situation:

* Do you regularly treat deep infiltrating endometriosis, especially involving the bowel or rectal area?

* Do you perform excision surgery, and how often do you handle repeat (redo) surgeries?

* If bowel involvement is suspected, do you work with a colorectal surgeon as part of your team?

* Do you use specialist endometriosis imaging (MRI or expert ultrasound) to map disease before treatment?

* How do you approach cases where pain is severe but pregnancy isn’t something the patient wants to prioritise right now?

These should give you a good sense of whether she’s equipped to manage a more complex case like yours.
Ok thank you for the help. I’ll keep this in mind and keep on looking depending on her answers.
Certainly. And you are more than welcome to come back to us if you need any additional assistance.

Thanks for letting us know you're looking for a pelvic physiotherapist or osteopath with experience in endometriosis care around Woking.

To help us find the best fit for you, could you tell us a little more about:

No need to rush, just share what you're comfortable with when you have a moment.

Diagnosed with endometriosis in November 2023, and diagnosed with PCOS in 2021. I have had 5 sessions of pelvic osteopathy whilst I was in Australia which helped massively, but symptoms have since worsened/returned

Knaphill, Woking. Prefer to be seen in surrounding areas nearby. 

I would be self funding.

Thanks so much for sharing all that detail – it's really helpful.

We can certainly look for pelvic physiotherapists or osteopaths in the Knaphill/Woking area who have experience with endometriosis and symptoms like pelvic pain, bloating, and bowel/bladder issues.

To help us narrow down the best options, could you tell us a little more about what you're hoping to achieve with this new practitioner? For example:

*   Are you looking for someone who offers a specific approach or technique?
*   Is there anything you particularly liked or disliked about your previous osteopathy sessions?
*   Are you looking for ongoing management, or more focused treatment for a specific issue?

I'm looking for a practitioner who is able to offer internal treatment.

I did briefly see a pelvic osteo in Guildford and I felt that the hands on time was not enough for the price of the sessions. 

Ongoing management
*treatment to support pelvic floor function
Please let me know if you need any further information

Hey there, based on everything you've shared, we can now start looking for pelvic physiotherapists or osteopaths in the Knaphill/Woking area who specialise in endometriosis care, offer internal treatment, and are geared towards ongoing support. If we need any more information we will let you know. 

We'll get back to you as soon as we have some potential options. Please bear with us while we do some research.

Hi — thanks for your patience while we reviewed this.

Based on what you’ve shared, we’ve focused on physiotherapists who specifically work in women’s health and pelvic pain, as that tends to be the most relevant approach for endometriosis-related symptoms.

We’ve shortlisted a few options and included a bit of context on why each may be a good fit — particularly around pelvic floor work and managing ongoing or chronic pain, which is often an important part of the picture.

Jo Coulter Physiotherapy

Tier: Excellent Fit

📍 Woking (Petrarch House, Horsell Park)

Why they may be a good fit:
This is the clearest match in your list. The profile explicitly mentions pelvic girdle pain and chronic/persistent pain, which is important — endometriosis-related pain often behaves like a chronic pain condition, not just a structural issue.

The “mind-body / pain recovery” angle is also relevant. Patients with longstanding endometriosis often develop central sensitisation, where the nervous system amplifies pain — and clinicians who understand this tend to manage outcomes better than purely mechanical physios.

For your situation — ongoing pelvic pain linked to endometriosis — this is the type of practitioner most likely to combine pelvic work with chronic pain management, rather than just treating symptoms locally.

Best for:
Patients with persistent pelvic pain, especially where pain hasn’t fully resolved after surgery or feels disproportionate to findings.

Pricing: n/a
🔗 http://www.jocoulter.com

Rodger Duckworth Physiotherapy Practice

Tier: Strong Option

📍 Wokingham (Berkshire) + remote available

Why they may be a good fit:
They explicitly list Women’s Health and also include myofascial release and cranial treatment, which suggests a broader, whole-body approach — useful when pelvic pain is linked to tension patterns, posture, or systemic pain.

However, what’s missing is clear, explicit mention of pelvic pain / pelvic floor internal work, which is usually key in endometriosis pathways. That doesn’t mean they don’t do it — just that it’s not strongly signposted.

For your situation — pelvic pain linked to endometriosis — this could still be helpful, but you’d want to confirm their experience with internal pelvic floor treatment and endo-specific cases.

Best for:
Patients open to a broader MSK + women’s health approach, especially with remote flexibility.

Pricing: n/a
🔗 http://www.rdphysio.com

Trinity Physiotherapy

Tier: Strong Option

📍 Multiple sites (Woking, Weybridge)

Why they may be a good fit:
They clearly offer Women’s Health Physiotherapy sessions, which is the right category — and being a multi-site clinic can make access easier.

That said, like many clinics, the profile is broad rather than specialist-led. There’s no clear signal of:

Endometriosis-specific experience
Chronic pelvic pain focus
Internal pelvic floor expertise

So this sits as a solid, practical option — but not the most targeted.

For your situation — if access and convenience matter — this is a reasonable starting point, but you may want to ensure you’re booked specifically with a women’s health physio who treats pelvic pain regularly.

Best for:
Accessible, general women’s health physio where convenience and availability matter.

Pricing: n/a
🔗 https://trinityphysiotherapy.co.uk/

Nuffield Health Physiotherapy (Multiple Sites)

Tier: Strong Option

📍 Nationwide (including Newcastle, Leeds, York, etc.)

Why they may be a good fit:
Nuffield is one of the few large providers that consistently offers women’s health physiotherapy, including pelvic floor rehab.

The strength here is structure and consistency — you’re more likely to get:

Proper assessment pathways
Integration with consultants if needed
Easier escalation if symptoms persist

The trade-off is that it’s less personalised and less specialist-led than an independent pelvic pain physio.

For your situation — this is a reliable option, especially if you want something closer to home or integrated with a hospital setting.

Best for:
Patients who want a structured, hospital-linked physio pathway with easier access locally.

Pricing: n/a
🔗 https://www.nuffieldhealth.com/physiotherapy

Would be great to get your sense on these — do any of them feel like a good fit for you?

If helpful, we can also help you draft a quick message to send to their clinic to confirm things like pelvic floor experience or whether they regularly treat endometriosis-related pain, so you can be confident before booking.

And if you’d prefer, we can also adjust the search — either more specialist or closer to you.

Thanks for sharing all these details with us. We're now looking into suitable specialists for your complex case and will get back to you shortly with some recommendations.

Given how complex your case is, we’ve focused on surgeons who are experienced in deep infiltrating endometriosis involving the bowel and bladder, while also trying to keep things as close to home as possible where it makes sense.

Mr Samendra N Roy

Tier: Excellent Fit

📍 Middlesbrough / Stockton-on-Tees

Why they may be a good fit:
Mr Roy is one of the strongest local options for advanced endometriosis excision, with explicit experience in deep infiltrating disease and ureterolysis, alongside performing complex laparoscopic hysterectomy. His profile shows a clear surgical focus on multi-organ involvement, which is critical given bowel and bladder symptoms.

For your situation — deep infiltrating endometriosis affecting the bladder and bowel, with prior incomplete surgery — having a surgeon who is comfortable operating across these areas and not stopping at “unremovable disease” is particularly important. His proximity also makes him a very practical option for ongoing care and follow-up.

Best for:
Patients in the North East seeking a locally accessible but surgically capable excision specialist for complex disease.

Pricing: £150 (new) / £133 (follow-up)
🔗 https://www.nuffieldhealth.com/consultants/mr-somendra-roy

Mr Chin Hooi Gan

Tier: Excellent Fit

📍 Elland (West Yorkshire) / Harrogate / Grimsby

Why they may be a good fit:
Mr Gan has explicit experience in advanced complex endometriosis, including rectovaginal disease and bowel resection, which directly matches this level of severity. His procedural list clearly includes deep disease excision involving the bowel, which is a key differentiator for cases like this.

For your situation — bowel urgency after eating, bladder involvement, and previous surgeons unable to remove lesions — this level of radical excision capability is highly relevant. He is also experienced in hysterectomy, but within the context of comprehensive disease management, rather than positioning it as a standalone solution.

Best for:
Patients with confirmed or suspected bowel-involving endometriosis needing a surgeon comfortable with full excision, not just superficial treatment.

Pricing: £250 (new) / £150 (follow-up)
🔗 https://www.spirehealthcare.com/spire-elland-hospital/consultants/mr-chin-hooi-gan-c6050076

There is a BSGE-accredited centre in Newcastle, but it appears that it primarily operates through the NHS pathway. That means access is typically via referral and waiting lists, rather than direct private booking.

I had a diagnostic lap in 2017 which confirmed it and a large endometrioma drained. I had a further surgery in 2018 to excise fhe endometrioma and since then have been relatively pain free. I have recently been dealing with pain again and am currently on a long waiting list to be seen which is 12 months wait. 
I am located in Dy6 and I am open to travel. 
I have insurance with my work provider but they put menstrual health on my exclusions because I was having trouble at the time. So it would be self funding. 
Ideally I need nhs and want to see if I have other options aside from Birmingham women’s where I am currently waiting. 
Thank you

*   What does the pain feel like, and where is it located?

Thank you. 
I get left sided stabbing pain on and off on my left side which I believe could be from having a large endometrioma removed and my ovary now being stuck by my pouch of Douglas. 
I get on and off sharp pelvic pain at times that is relieved form bowel movements but this flares up every now and then. 
But my main concern is since my period end of November I have been having daily pain in my coccyx, rectum, vagina. It feels like heavy pain, aching, stiffness, tightness and pressure. This varies day to day and I find it hard to sit down. 
It intensifies during my period. And can differ day to day but is always there. 
I tend to take paracetamol mostly as I can’t have Ibupophen. The doctor has prescribed codeine and amityptaline but I haven’t yet taken these. 
I have been referred to Birmingham women’s and have had a lumbar mri which they are repeating in a couple of weeks. I’ve had a colposcopy which came back clear and an internal ultrasound which showed my ovary being stuck. 
Thank you

Hi there,

This is Jack from DocMap. Thanks so much for getting back to us and for sharing more about your situation. It sounds like you've been through a lot with your endometriosis, and we really hear your frustration with the NHS waiting times – that's completely understandable.

It's clear you're looking for a highly skilled specialist for excision surgery, and we can definitely help connect you with experts who handle complex Stage 4 cases, including those considering a hysterectomy.

To help us narrow down the best specialists for you, could you tell us a little more about:

*   **Your previous surgeries**: What kind of surgeries were they (e.g., ablation, excision)? When were they, and what were their outcomes?

Once we have a bit more detail, we can start looking into specialists who are a good fit. We can also discuss how people typically manage funding for private care, as there are often different routes to explore.

We're here to help you find the right path forward.
